author: @Escapist...
comment:
I honestly liked every episode and how every social issue was tackled by the show, even at the cost of being "preachy".
The one with the Fair-skin obsession hit specially close to home. I live in Delhi and my own Cousin (with somewhere between wheatish to dusky complexion and beautiful big eyes, she is the prettiest Girl I swear to God) was taking some wedding make-up package from a very well known Salon for 3 months. We never really understood why she had to in the first place but as distant cousins, we had no say. It was Love marriage where there was no pressure from her in-laws to look a certain way (at least none that we could see). 
Her skin broke out the day before her Mehendi. My God, what a nightmare it was for the whole family with some even contemplating postponing the wedding. Her Father threatened legal action on the Salon, who had the audacity to say "Had you taken one-year package we would have more time to study your skin and suggest and improvise products accordingly." 
Ultimately, She got married on the day as was planned. The Salon people did her make-up but to cover up the marks, they lathered her face with too too much make-up. She did not look herself, did not feel herself, was self-conscious the entire wedding and sadly, couldn't enjoy her own day. 
What irks me the most is the lack of Sensitivity some people have in our family. Some people watching the wedding album would comment "I told you so", "What was the need?", making her re-visit her ordeal.

author: @aditichoudhury3425
comment:
Saaf ho jaayega? Bloody kichad lagaa hai kya ? Jo saaf ho jaayegaa ? 
        She is so beautiful already! Her perfect v shaped eyebrows and big sharp eyes will make her look like a goddess after makeup especially in bridal makeup. Can we all stop falling for these nonsense treatments.  
            And this is coming from a dusky skinned girl, who till now hears derogatory comments about my skintone, have been compared with my mom because she is fair and I am not, has a lot of self-esteem issues because of constant putting down due to skin tone, etc.      But still I would never do these treatments to make my skin shade lighter. I would rather work on my personality, confidence that will heal me.        And we are the biggest hypocrite, we worship Krishna ji, Kali maa, even Draupadi was dark skinned, etc but bully people who are not fair.

author: @ranjana240
comment:
True. This is the prevailing truth. They always show Hindus in bad light and so many of our traditions are shown as negative or regressive practices which is disheartening. No one wants to know the beautiful and meaningful origins of these traditions and only want to be modern and cool by boycotting them (eg: kanyadaan) 

Muslims and Christians also have caste systems, there are atrocities in these communities as well. Muslims are so terrible to their women, all their traditions are to suppress women and their creative abilities and somehow no one wants to talk about that.
A friend of mine who’s Muslim- hasn’t clicked a photo of hers coz it’s haram, loves movies but stopped watching after age 14 coz it’s haram, never visits or socialises with any of her non Muslim friends out of family gatherings coz it’s haram. I don’t know how is this progressive. So many other friends have similar stories. 

In contrast to all of my Hindu friends- all are working or doing business, they have freedom to wear what they want, we socialise, we go to each other’s houses and help out whenever needed. Their husbands and mine treat us as equals. Most of us never got into dowry discussions and hence our weddings were beautiful. Where are the real stories and why is the characterisation so polar? I don’t get it.

author: @lakshmikrithika2521
comment:
@akashpatre8643 I mean even the people who you call lower class aren’t exactly jumping when you tell them you are marrying somebody from another caste! 
People connect with familiarity. And they feel familiarity. A caste is such sort of familiarity. 

Try telling a SC parents they’re marrying a ST person. Waise hi dange ho jaaye. 
so it’s not an issue with upper vs lower caste. 
It’s also lower vs lower caste! 

Har baat pe upper caste ko leke chaati Pete a band kardo. Bahut outdated cheez ho gayi hai. Ab toh bhaav Dena bhi band ho gaya hai.

Even I didn’t want to marry outside of my caste. But I married outside of my sun caste and I already feel different with my in-laws regarding food and rehne ka tareeka and customs. The customs I loved most are not even there in my in-laws. Kuch aur hi karte hai ye log. Not that they’re not accepting but they’re not excited I would be with my family and similar caste people. So again familiarity brings a sense of security. 

America mai is liye toh Indians itne acche se rehte hai because they seem the wide spectrum but within that they see the Indian familiarity and feel safe.

author: @prasad13mane
comment:
That Dalit Wedding - When I saw the picture of Radhika Apte in a White Saree I immediately guessed it must be a Buddhist wedding. I posted same on the Instagram stating, I hope this is a Buddhist wedding. However, one of my friend commented this cannot be a Buddhist wedding as she is wearing "Nath, a maharashtrian ornament" so this must be a Maharashtrian wedding to which I schooled him Maharashtrian Buddhist Brides do wear Nath at their weddings. Do you see how people have zero knowledge about Buddhist/Dalit wedding here? But when the trailer confirmed it, I danced joyfully and even taunt a little to my friend. However, it wasn't his fault. Like you said this is the first time Buddhist wedding has been shown on any major platform. Forget about the wedding, this is first time any Buddhist tradition has been shown on Major Platform. Forget about any traditions I don't even see a Buddhist friend in all these television serials or movies.. And of course, it takes Neeraj Ghaywan to do it..
Almost everything shown it that episode is true.. Dalit's changing their surname to hide their identity, adjusting during inter-caste weddings, feeling guilty about whom they are, everything..
We are in 2023 and I still see this happening around me.. 
Last week, I witnessed an inter-caste marriage between a Buddhist Boy and Hindu Girl where not a single Buddhist ritual was followed and the boy's "real identity" was hidden from the bride's entire family.

author: @nishant_kharkwal
comment:
Good thing: Focus on social issues especially domestic violence (gas lighting) and Dalit upper caste wedding issues. Though I have some reservations with the storyline. 
First of all there is no such so called Dalit wedding. Dalits live in entire parts of the country and have different customs of there own. Further, the custom shown in the episode 5 of the season 2 is a Navyana Buddhist Dalit custom of wedding, which mostly happens in Maharashtra.  The essence of Dalitness was not even understood properly by the makers themselves. Though I appreciate that they bring such an important social issue into the limelight. further I do not understand what is such obsession of Joya with English. I think the season 2 was for the International audience especially for the NRIS but not for the Indian one. Further, I do not understand why the so much focus on broken relationships and obsession with extramarital relationships and casual sex with anyone. Or either I do not relate to the world of so rich portrait by makers of the series.

author: @KPA-iu9wz
comment:
Also when Bhoppana says he did not hide Meher’s story because he did not see the need to, it was disgusting. He was trying to show that he’s so not bigoted and doesn’t care who knows her reality.  He can be as unbigoted as he wants, but her story is not his to tell. She will tell whom she wants to when she wants to. The film makers did wrong by sexual minorities by doing this. Yes, someone’s sexuality should not matter and who we are with should be normalized, but the straight community does not sit around discussing their favorite positions either.  Intimate details are nobody’s business but the couples. When people don’t think they have to pat themselves on the back for being with a sexual minority is when it is normalized. 

I also hated the wedding between the two lesbians. Why did they have to be on horses looking like fools?  Why do they have to be outlandish?  They are as normal as the next person. 

The Muslim woman trying to kill herself was ridiculous. She did not want to leave because she wouldn’t get custody of her kids, but by killing herself, somehow her kids would be protected?  Yes, polygamy is deeply painful and could be challenged. What was awful however, was the way wife number two was flaunted by the husband and his family - that was the true crime.  If it was banned, how would Hema and Dharmendra’s daughters be legal? And how would this same film fraternity and Indian public that has voted them into the Lok Sabha give them the respect that they do?

In the Dalit story, they brought up a good point - the sister did not have the right to out her brother.  She was using her privilege as an educated and lauded academic over him. That hypocrisy was never explored, and the brother simply showed up at the wedding. 

The entire series was insane - why would a woman who was badly beaten marry the abuser? Why would her family stand by her instead of putting the man in jail? Why wouldn’t the woman be in therapy and learn to be strong? 

With the Bollywood couple, why continue the sham marriage? Why wouldn’t the bride walk away, talk about what the groom did to the press and completely screw him over? 

Why in every single story was the woman wronged? She was not fair enough, she was being married for money, she was beaten, she was a Dalit, she was abused…

Finally when is Mathur’s character going to get therapy? Yes, his mom abused him, yes he’s scared, get help instead of using addiction to sex and substances. Use your platform to normalize getting mental health services.

author: @shreydee14
comment: I thought it was a good watch! I honestly didn’t have a problem with the “lessons” each episode was drilled with. We’re living in a country where same-sex marriage is facing a ban, we’ve just had women being paraded naked on streets in Manipur, beaten raped and abused and there’s so much more happening in our country. As modern and progressive minded as people seem to be in urban set ups, the show actually points out their inherent regression and patriarchy. That even if you’re an educated, rich person driving a Mercedes Benz living in a high rise apartment or a mansion, you’re not free from judging people very different from you. You judge them, ostracise them, and feel disgust. Your education didn’t make a difference but your upbringing did. A show like this very succinctly makes you think and I really felt that Zoya and Reema’s best intentions were brought in here. I didn’t feel like this was inclusion for the sake of inclusion. How many times have people seen Dalit marriages in movies or shows? How many of us have seen same sex wedding vows exchange on Hindi shows. Karan spiralling is a reality for someone who’s mother is dying and doesn’t want to see him anymore or Tara trying to salvage as much as she can is a reality that one can’t hide from if she’s divorcing from a high profile arrogant husband who cheated on her. This is reality and it’s been portrayed in the most sanitised way possible.
title: 4 Year Wait For This?  | Made In Heaven Season 2 Review | Amazon Prime Video

author: @OnionTurtle_
comment:
MIH depicts the 5% population of India with the rich and show off culture who talks English only to yap around to sound "educated".... Coming to the storyline, yes there were some serious issues like dark skin, physical abuse, dalit wedding, lesbian wedding, polygamy, etc that were portrayed well.... What was missing was the emotions behind every wedding... It seemed like nobody is really happy from inside, they're just doing it to maintain a family-social image.... Many of the weddings showed how mostly the women compromised the shortcomings and decided to move ahead even after knowing it's consequences.... Call me cold, but I could not shed a single teardrop because I dint feel connected throughout the series.... 

However, I loved Mona Shah's role in this as a worried mother and her acting skills kept me hooked up for a while.... I also liked the trans girl's story and way of thinking....Perhaps she was the only character in the entire series who was clean with no double strings going along side by side.... I dint at all connect with Kabir-Jaz's romance though....The lite version of Sharukh and Jaz going to the mechanic for emotional support totally lost me.... All in all, I was just waiting for this series to get over ASAP and I even checked in between how many episodes are left.... 

I would therefore recommend the filmmakers and the viewers to focus on real life based content and work around it because this series is only for the 5% population that survives on killing their inner selves by pleasing the world by creating fake identities for themselves.... This is not the world a regular human likes to live in... By showing off your richness even at the cost of selling your souls and morals... It is perhaps very demeaning to watch the high class and the rich to even follow these kind of ridiculous customs on dark skin, polygamy, physical abuse, etc....

author: @chiravuris
comment: @@Nonabacha43 Wait till you see the reality of class division in rural India. Now, I haven’t gone through anything, I’m a Brahmin too. But I’m dad works in a specialty that involves traveling to rural India a lot. The discrimination you see there is the worst. It’s not even about shame. It’s not treating them as even human beings. People complain about series that show this inequality that they’re not realistic. IT IS realistic. It’s just that the people that complain belong to urban India. These people are not even allowed to sit down or wear shoes (as per what my dad said, he was shocked at how people are treated). We don’t believe in untouchability and all that regressive crap. Treating each other with kindness is all god cares for and wants. No point in doing 10 hours of poojas if you can’t treat another human being with kindness. Shame is the least of the problems.

author: @suhas274
comment:
Even I noticed that MIH is just reduced to social commentary instead of showing how tough is arranging grand wedding from logistics point of view.

Karan gets away with stealing 18 lacs from MIH.

Tara has problem when she sees bride getting dominated by husband/in laws/family but she's fine to carry on getting dominated by her own mother.

Why Kabir is projected as SRK of MIH team is beyond me. I'm not sure which wedding cinematographers have MIH directorial team seen to show Kabir wearing casuals in ceremonies.

Jazz feels entitled about everything.

Meher, a "progressive" individual having regressive idea about finding love on dating app.

Adil ke ghar mein toh like father like son and history repeating itself ke kisse chal rahe hai. Adil is an industrialist who keeps underestimating his competitor Tara in divorce settlement even after being made aware of how she tricked him into getting married to her in first place.

Har koi ismein khud ko entitled feel karta hai.

author: @IndusBee
comment:
Stark contrast in Ramesh Jauhari s character his mention of Bulbul Jauhari as one of Adil Khanna's Company shareholders taps into the strong characterisation of the same.only saving grace is Radhika Apte as accomplished Dalit Professor & Mrinal Thakur stringed to the beast fiance out of toxic love.

Being woke comes across as a conscious choice but after getting betrayed and harbouring guilt of gaining & loosing a trophy wife status ,which was pre-planned by Tara it doesn't spare room for conservatism.Her transgressions obviously bore audience as that is not what they want to see.Tara, giving strong reply to Raghav stresses on her changed priorities more evident now. Although  lost as a woman,she doesn't loose confidence. Seeing her ex- husband getting a baby with a divorcee ex-friend entitled her to change her soft spot & it's supported by the fact when Adil's mom offers her 18 lac favour asking for a return favour....As you sow ,so you reap....!

Roman & Jules's story is a breath of fresh air. Some aren't ordained companions by heaven; they simply must learn to be their own, live in the hope that no feeling is final ... you need to have felt that pain to say yes I've earned the right to write a review! It's not just the thoughts , season 2 resonated with me on many levels...loved it .
title: 4 Year Wait For This?  | Made In Heaven Season 2 Review | Amazon Prime Video

author: @ishantrana8003
comment:
When Tara said in last episode "Stop telling me what i deserve" , i got that, ok her character is flawed and show gives u that "get after what u want, don't be pretentious" vibe but than what is the point of being a protagonist when u can't rise from the lows that u created. she was my fav character in S1 this season she lost her originality even more...in S1 despite her mother brainwashing her into becoming a gold digger, she was atleast contemplating on what she did to Adil and how Karma unfolded and she left their home whereas with some dignity but in this season she was all about i will treat everyone on the basis of how they treat me...i mean then what's the point of being a protagonist...
Arjun on the other hand i related with, i feel like being gay i was also exposed to the dark side of the gay world which is about hookups, drugs, showoff and all kinds of vices...i pulled myself back in a very short period of time but i was responsible for what happened to me, while the show's whole focus was on how everyone's doings are a result of their upbringing and they just operate from the hollow their childhood has given them...I relate wid ur point on the S1 exploring societal ills with the main characters but this season brides and grooms were sort of shown as the main characters and the wokeness in the caste episode was over the top, when Trinetra said to Radhika "i love ur work on intersectionality of caste and gender"...i mean most of the people dont even know wtf it means moreover when the makers will be accused to being leftists they will portray themselves as centrists...and i laughed hysterically when in last episode the girl took vows with herself.

author: @nirvana9891
comment:
Season 2 left me somewhat dissatisfied. It occasionally came across as overly preachy. Unlike the previous season, the weddings felt less realistic and more extravagant. However, there were some redeeming aspects:

Tara and Karan were portrayed as flawed characters, a refreshing departure from the tendency to justify all actions of female protagonists. Tara's character was skillfully depicted, highlighting the influence of her mother on her mindset, made her a relatable yet flawed protagonist. Similarly, Karan's character remained authentic, as seen in the previous season when he grappled with self-destruction due to his strained relationship with his mother.
The episodes addressing domestic violence and the obsession with fair skin were the season's strong points, reflecting real-life issues. Many women believe love can “cure” abusive behavior , even though it's not the right perspective. However, they did show Mrunal's character taking legal action against her abusive husband. The portrayal of the Dalit wedding felt somewhat superficial, as if it were merely checking a "Dalit issues" box.

Bulbul Jouhri was the standout character of this season.

I was surprised by Jazz’s selection of outfits and how was she actually affording it? She was mostly speaking in English in this season. Between the two seasons did she take a secret lesson in the same School that Tara went to? The excessive use of English throughout the season was a headache-inducing factor.

Things that really touched my heart in this series:
- bulbul johari character and her family arc
- the ending scene of episode 2 ( of domestic abuse) was so heartbreaking yet impactful.. everyone was sad that the bride was going to suffer but couldn't do anything... specially when Mona Singh started crying..so emotional
- Buddhist wedding was so ethereal.. beautifully shot and narrated towards the end
- Meher's arc
- last but not the least it was Karan who stole the limelight, despite of all the mess he was going through he kept his empathy and sensitivity towards others specially hos interaction with the little boy and how he saved Diya Mirza and gave her strength .. his character showed that no matter how much weak we are, if we keep our humanity, we can  be a hero in somebody's life :)

Overall I liked this season as well.. looking forward to season 3

author: @dOVERanalyst
comment:
​ @TriedRefusedProductions It was bizzare. You're bang on. You feel for Adil and Faiza but until Adil cheats with Tara again.

The ONLY time you feel for Tara in the ENTIRE SEASON is when she stares at the baby pram being carved. THAT hurt her. Not the cheating.
When she'd left, that dialogue had so much impact.
The marriage isn't working. I cheated. You cheated. 
That was gold.
She was self aware even if she was evil, she'd got her karmic balance, it seemed.
But now? The way she found love, happiness but is obsessed with money. 
Karan was even worse than Tara. HOW can someone root for him?
What scene was supposed to humanize him?
If anything, you hate him.

On such a large scale it perpetuates that women marry for divorce, that all are gold diggers! Even when they're the ONES BEING TORTURED.
That hero was clearly playing games!
To gaslight her like that?

It seemed like the impossible weddings went through just so that they could get their money!!

In a way we are like Karan's mother on a deathbed wondering HOW do you expect us to root for you? How?
You take advantage of EVERY man you date. Even that 5 lakh loan giving fashion designer. 
Like what was his fault?
How did he make you "uncomfortable" by trying to listen to your problems that you just leave him like that?

From one to the other. No feelings. No emotions. No loyalty. Why do we need to see their sexual lives? Anyone's?
I just wanted story. It sounds so good in pitch. 
Lavish wedding management. And drama.
But WHERE IS the drama?

author: @poulomi__hari
comment:
MIH season 2 was very long awaited and to be fair, it wasn't as good as the first one. But it wasn't bad. I get your POV, it can come off as a snoozefest to male audience as it is written from the POV of a woman. I however do feel that they overdid a lot of things in this season. 
Jazz's storyline in last season was super boring to me. In this season it manages to take it a notch higher. The "open relationship" checkbox. 
Tara and Adil's divorce had a pleasent beginning but Tara's girlboss side wakes up and she does terrible things just out of spite. Her rebound with the chef was a complete filler. 
Adil remains a pig throughout. Meher's story portrays various trans issues. 
The weddings are now not as fun as they used to be in season 1. There was humor that was thrown at the viewer with the help of wedding stories where the upper class brides were more or less bridezillas. However this season we get all the super mature girlbosses standing up against societal norms. 

The only story arc that gave me some kind of satisfaction was Karan's story. I loved the track so much, especially with the cameo of Vikrant Massey's character. I loved it that they didn't make them have an affair. Karan was dealing with grief and Nawab was there for him during the pivotal moments. I loved the fact that this time their relationship was shown in a non-sexual way which gives a lot of depth to both of them. The intimacy of thoughts they share is very commendable. It was wholesome. Nawab came for him without asking and Karan respected their boundaries. In their last scene when Nawab hugs him and Karan later sleeps in the sheet, masking in his scent... I thought it was a very poignant scene. This non-sexual affection honored the true love of these characters. Personally I believe Karan and Akshay had quite a chemistry this season and he must be given a chance to grow in the upcoming season if there is. Nawab is Karan's first love but I think Akshay can be his last love. 

Overall I loved the 5th episode the most. And not because of Radhika Apte, but the small kid whose divorced mamma was getting married again. I loved that secondary storyline (Ahuja Menon wedding). The boy was super sweet and seeing him accept a new father at the gateway after having struggled with the new arrangement, melted my heart. I remember having wept throughout that whole scene where the little boy, dressed in the veshti and standing at the front door  cordially welcomes his mother's groom. I absolutely adored that scene, it made me cry. And yes, the Buddhist wedding was my favourite in the season in terms of cinematography. 

This season was good, but it was a bit dull compared to the previous one because of its lack of humor. It is completely fine to portray girlbosses and woke concepts, but the audience doesn't have to be fed it down their throats. In the last season by introducing Vijay Raz and the various Bridezillas they kept the humor quotient alive while carefully weaving the societal struggles. I still remember the episode from season 1 where the bride was a pilot and her father in law a maharaja, but also a pedophile. The bride seemed to be ok with it and tried to cover up the scandal. I waited for stories like those where the hypocrisy of the world was exposed so well. This season lacked stories like those, essentially making all the brides of this season either victims or girl-bosses. Tara showed no growth and it breaks my heart to see such a magnanimous character being turned into a b***h. In last season Tara was a gold-digger who was reaping the spoils of her own karma as she witnessed her dream marriage shamble. In this season I expected her to stand up against her greedy mother and give up on her money-hungry patterns. I expected that she came back wiser and learned to find happiness in small things. I really hoped that she stopped worrying about comfort and instead opened up emotionally to her chef boyfriend. But in the last episode they ruined it showing Tara prefers lavishness more than love and thats sickening. Tara's character disappointed me so much I can't even comprehend what went wrong with the writers in this one.

author: @annweshasarkar-m7u
comment:
Hey Jammy, I know its late, and the buzz about MIH is almost dead, but I'd still like you to hear me out on why I loved the second season as much as I did the first

1. While the first season dealt with the shit that goes into making the day special, the second season deals with the shit that goes on in the lives of the people that work towards making your day special

2.The complicated relationship between Tara and her MIL. She loved Tara like her own daughter and then goes on to accept Faiza because she's pregnant. So that leaves you questioning if a DIL is treated like a daughter in our society

3. The fact that Tara's affair with Raghav weakens her divorce case raises significant questions on the perceptions of our society

4. Tara looks beyond her materialistic tendencies, she truely loves Raghav (does not break up with him on her lawyer's advice) which shows growth in her character. But in the end she asserts what she must without compromising. Which I think is a great character trait.

5. The show slightly hints at the fact that adil deserves neither Tara nor Faiza (once a cheater always a cheater)

6. Jazz and Kabir's relationship highlights disparities among people from different sections of society, where one is mordernized while the other has a rather traditional view on love, and how that complicates things

7. Jazz's parents dont want her to be married off to anyone, fearing that she would stop taking care of them. They touched on a middle class problem, and it was accurate

It shows how to portray hard truths without romanticizing them. However, beyond that and the Dalit wedding, much of this season was lacking in the subtlety and nuance that the writing in season 1 had, which is why it worked when characters did take a big stand, like leaving their wedding when asked for dowry. Those moments had so much more impact, in season 1, but not as much in season 2.

author: @vaaorun6475
comment:
Also i feel that few of other loopholes with the writing of this season are :

1) Story arc of karan with debt and his argument with tara is brushed off soo quickly under the carpet like there was a rush to move on with it, no explanations, no confrontations, even karan moving from his addictions like it was a fluke, how lightly  these arcs are brushed off

2) Arjun's relation with his previous landlord's daughter aka his "foster child" ... She notices his behavior but then there's no closure on that part

3) i feel that there's some biases when it comes to showing actions of an individual in either positive/negative arc... For eg i don't think in Ep3 Sanjay kapoor and the other lady deseved to have left like that and yet their partner's infidelity was shown as "Union of Love" Blah blah... I guess it comes on to us what we as humans justify or don't, according to our own convienence. 

4) Also in S1...MIH was shown as two planners who involuntarily get caught up in between personal conflicts of marriages.. And they have to find a way to deal with them but...in this season they seem like social workers out on a mission to fight every injustice in marriages. Why would any marriage planner get involved voluntarily while going out of their way for clients... Is MIH a new NGO

5) Characters like Jazz and Kabir's arc seemed like extension love story that wasn't needed.. The attention it was given in S1 was appropriate this one seemed like a stretch to their personal arcs rather than connecting them to either with a  social commentary/overall story

6) Best for the last..or shall i say worst for the last...The ending seemed too rushed up and the last marriage story was such a disaster... The forced amount of positivity in its ending scene when the bride takes those vows to herself.. I think realistically or logically, there would be crickets at such a ceremony.. And then every character's arc for so rushed away with soo quickly.. 

PS : Kudos to Vikrant Massey and Ishwaq singh for their absolutely charming performances.. ✨

author: @persipony7008
comment:
​ @lavanyakrishnakumar7233 
I think it is very realistic how they portrayed it. You don't talk much during the process of divorce. Tara's only source of guidance was her own selfish mother. At first Tara is settling maybe out of guilt.

author: @nostalgicgirrl6053
comment: The makers did the exact opposite of representation and awareness-building. They used each social minority as a token character and made them one-dimensional, and as if they are only there to preach to the masses. There was no depth, no personal connection, no relatability. Conversations and confrontations between the characters that needed to happen were completely avoided, and what we got instead were lectures that sounded inauthentic to the script. Jazz had lost her humility, Kabir lost his creative edge, Tara couldn't showcase her rooted mindset, Karan behaved like an insufferable prick with zero self-awareness, and the new character - Mehr had no business being there other than being transgender and making the worst and immature dating decisions for a better part of the show. The only refreshing and noticeably authentic part of the script was Bulbul and Jauhari. The most hard-hitting and real story arc was the one involving their family's past and present situation.

author: @HELLBOY583
comment: This season tried way too hard to drive home social messages (that are anyway not novel any longer), and the effort showed. Some of the ends were also loose and were not tied up satisfactorily. Like why did Yashaswini's character flit in and out like a photo-bomber, when Tara says once that she is as good as Karan's foster daughter? Also, wtf happened, after Samir Soni's and Neelam's characters eloped, to their respective spouses and how did they deal with this fucked-up mess? I simply didn't buy that the wedding planners just happily ended up planning the lovebirds' wedding while the actual wedding was in a shambles. The only bit I found interesting and was invested in was Bulbul's tribulations involving her teenaged son. And I doubt if Adil or Faiza will ever speak a word of Hindi in this series (upcoming seasons if there are any).

author: @anantsharma6365
comment: @sumanparmar27  IMO the core reason for Adil's cheating is his privilege. He was born with a silver spoon, got everything he ever wanted, most probably got away with every wrong thing he ever did.....that privilege is what made him cheat repeatedly without thinking about the consequences. He is an Ahole bcoz of his privilege but IMO he is inherently not a bad person....key word being inherently. Like he would try to escape consequence if he killed someone in an accident, but he probably won't deliberately try to kill someone & make it look like an accident. On the other hand I consider Tara to be an inherently evil person who will go to any possible means to get what she wants even if it means deliberately killing someone. She is rude to her family just bcoz she thinks now she is better than them just bcoz she belongs to a different social strata of the society. She constantly advises other women to behave in a similar fashion & chase the same things which she did even though those other women are already in a very well to do position in their lives. She is not just a person of her circumstances i.e. poverty, but is inherently problematic. She has that mindset of money being above everything else & she would do anything for it. Similarly Adil has the mindset of "I am Adil & I can get what I want." For u it might be different but I consider both of them equally sh*tty. In fact Adil has been shown to feel guilty of his actions in a few scenes, but the only time Tara has been shown to acknowledge her wrong doings is when she became the one who got cheated on. I don't feel sympathetic at all for what Adil gets to go through, but I do feel irritated at Tara almost never facing any consequences for her actions. Just getting divorce from a sh*tty husband is not big enough of a consequence IMO.

author: @avikgupta44
comment:
I feel this season has become more of a corporate presentation of societal challenges rather than a coherent entertaining show, like the season one.

Most of the marriages shown this season are superfluous and lack the emotional connect (except Mrunal Thakur and Radhika Apte’s stories - it is beautiful to see a Buddhist weeding on screen). Moreover, don’t know if you feel like this but isn’t it too much to believe that each family’s problems are getting solved with ease once a pep talk or two is given by Tara or any other lead characters? I mean come on, you are showing posh South Delhi people and making us believe these guys will get influenced and change their life changing decisions just by done-to-death conversations with some random professional wedding planners? 

However, I love how Tara’s character has evolved into sort of evil one and on the other hand with each episode Faiza and Adil become more likeable. Bulbul Johari, gracefully played by an amazing Mona Singh, and her section of the story is undoubtedly the most engaging part. Rest of the players are, however, failed to bring in the expected nuances on the table. 

I believe makers were to keen to make the show an wholesome experience by packing all the stuff they could from social commentary to personal story of each character to large scale scenes to grandeur. Unfortunately, while packing so many punches all together, they missed the novelty of season one. May be, this one is NOT Made in Heaven.

author: @priyamvada-11
comment: Zoya’s work lately has implicit anti-Hindu hatred. Made in Heaven S1 was a well-written show, no doubt and I am sure S2’s writing will be good as well. But S1 had a subtle anti-Hindu bigoted undertone. All the societal problems like dowry, opposition to inter-religious marriage, patriarchy, opposition to homosexuality, etc were all shown in Hindu families and only Hindu characters were on the wrong side of the debate. That’s not all; she also implicitly compares this with other communities where in she shows the characters from other communities on the right side of the debate - for example, Karan’s family is shown to have issues with his homosexuality but not his boyfriend’s (who comes from a Muslim family); Rasika Duggal’s character’s Hindu family is shown to be extremely vile and opposed to her marriage to her Christian boyfriend but the boyfriend’s family, in contrast is shown to be very ‘open-minded’ and all welcoming. This is not a one of instance - such portrayals have been a trend in entertainment industry since decades now - which implicit and very cunningly bias people against Hindus and show Hindus as inferior to other communities. I believed that Shantanu was good at catching such bigotry but am a little disappointed that he hasn’t been able to see that lately.

author: @akshitaravish
comment: As also mentioned in the review - reservation . considering the story line , it was made hugely stereotypical . the fact that in the scene where she is giving an interview and a question is asked on the character's view point over reservations , she says it made her father achieve education and rise up and then she and her brother are also deemed fit to receive the same quota . this wholly defies the social upliftment norm , uplifting the uplifted ? now as we might say that reservations are based on social status of a caste and social discrimination , it might sound correct on the forefront for them to use the quota . but seeing the disparity , I thing educational reservation is okay to provide them a chance to equal opportunity but in professional life , it makes no sense . what you become in life should entirely be based on merit and that would be equality . you cannot help someone cheat an exam , you can only provide them with knowledge without any disparity .

author: @reflectsonlife
comment:
@su4ita
So according to you, MIH S2 was too full of woke messages... but Rocky aur Rani was not?  I invite you to rewatch the final 30 minutes of RaRkPH, from the almost-slap till the end. Every single line is KJo lecturing the audience about Patriarchy. My mind was so numb by the end that I could no longer process the messaging. As the saying goes, KJo doesn't know how to end a film. 
At least MiHS2 had amazing storylines and actors for the wedding planners. Bulbul actress and storyline is something you almost never see on film, and the trans storyline had hyperspecific scenarios that you never see in Indian film - a welcome change from the usual "coming out" or "self acceptance" or "being beat up or killed for being different" PSA-style storylines that hetero media usually affords lgbtq.
My main complaint about MIHS2 is that it didn't have any lighter wedding episodes like S1 had.  I enjoyed having the mix in S1. Like the comic episode of the bride fangirling over movie star (and kissing or sleeping with him right b4 the wedding). Or the not-comedic but relatively-light episode about the bride marrying the tree b4 the wedding despite the groom's objection.  I wish S2 had some lighter wedding episodes like those, not every wedding needed to have a heavy- handed issue to deal with. Also, I was not rooting for sobhita to win anything substantial in the divorce settlement, given that she had broken up Jim and Kalki's initial romance in the first place. So it felt like one of those dark movies where we watch the villain (not antihero) win, like Michael Caine in "A Shock to the System".
title: Made In Heaven Season 2 REVIEW | Sucharita Tyagi | Zoya Akhtar | Amazon Prime Video

author: @sayantrivedi
comment:
I think keeping Arjun and Shobita's characters morally ambiguous is a great decision. Their grey shades make them more human and interesting to me, and they are still likeable because most of the times they do realize what they are doing and try to make up.
I completely agree with you on the english dialogue part and the last episode though. And yes they probably chew a tad too much than they can swallow. But I enjoyed the commentary at the end by Shashank however generic it might seem, probably because I am too passionate about the issues those stories and  the commentary portray.
Rooting for Adil and Faiza is I think a success for the makers. This is not a typical bollywood movie where there is a typical antagonist whom you should hate.
Also regarding the character of Trinetra, I think she has experienced so much in her life that small setbacks like being thrown out of a car do not affect her that much any more. I felt that and hence that seemed realistic to me. She was shown as a strong and resilient woman.
Also I really loved that Arjun and his mother never reconciled even when she was on death bed. And then the conversation between Arjun and Vikrant was real gold.
title: 4 Year Wait For This?  | Made In Heaven Season 2 Review | Amazon Prime Video

NO SPOILERS but this season she did bizarre morally corrupt shit. And by the end of it she was still a good person at heart.

Though i loved mona singh’s character and trinetra’s character (only people who stood out this season)

author: @weirdo221B
comment:
When a rich kid buys a private medical seat, noone calls them undeserving!! But when an oppressed community's kid get a seat, people call it undeserving!!
 I will share 2 small stories, please read- 
1) I have a junior (SC category). Her father is Auto driver and she lives in a 2 room house with Tin as a roof. Her father couldn't afford coaching fees also, so she joined only 1 class for physics, rest chemistry and Biology she did on her own. And she got a MBBS seat in Govt medical College. (SC category seat)
2) My friend who is a single child and both his parents are doctors. He is open category but he couldn't get a good rank so his parents bought a MBBS seat in Deemed (private) University. He completed his MBBS and now his parents bought a MD seat for him also...so he is currently persuing his MD in Private clg.
 Now tell me who is deserving and who is undeserving??

author: @Talesvilla
comment:
Season 1 was definitely a masterpiece. It was like visually watching a best written novel written on modern India and it was concluded in such a beautiful way. That  scene of Tara wearing all the jewellery in the last episode of season 1 had such a deep meaning about the whole journey of her character in that season and when she delivered  that speech to those girls in her old grooming school to not lose their individuality atleast. The lead characters relate themselves with the weddings and sometimes dive into their past lives with that beautiful background score - I loved it all. Now coming back to season 2 , it seemed to me the lead characters lost that substance from inside of them. It seemed to me like whole season was written in a rush and that is such a joke considering they took 4 years. Each and every wedding of season 1 left something deep in your heart but this season it was all preachy. And yes “ultra wokism” is killing this show too. 

Karan stealing 18 lakh from his own company and then having no consequences was simply a lazy writing. Deep down if you think about these characters you can still justify their actions ( Tara was so hurt , Karan mother saying you are killing me , I did not raise a girl ) but still “something” definitely missing from  the core of these characters or may be our  expectations were really high after such a great original season. 

Bulbul Jee was the best thing about this season and sadly Trinetra can’t act with same expressions in everything. 

Hopefully they return to original writers and director and their basic roots of season 1 for the next season.

I will rate this season 7/10 considering first one was 9.5/10 ❤

author: @rinila207
comment:
If we compare with season 1 then we’ll always be stuck in that loop. Although it’s normal to have some expectations of consistency, I believe each season should have its own identity and focus. They presented as much as they could between  60-75 minutes in terms of highlighting the issues of society. 

One thing I’ve noticed, when people usually complain about wokeness, what they’re honestly complaining about is not being able to use language, ideologies, perceptions, etc. that were once acceptable. E.g. In episode 5 (Radhika Apte one), Tara is refers to the grooms ceremony as the “main wedding” for which Meher calls her out for to which Tara brushes it off and says, “ You know what I mean”. Words matter! Words have an affect on people. Honestly people criticizing progressive speech are just lazy thinkers and lack critical thinking.

Just to be clear, I’m not saying Anmol is like that, but I’m basing more off on my experience with others and some commentators here.

author: @putul8963
comment:
Tara always valued money over family, except when it came to a child. Season 1 showed us the lengths she would go for getting what she wanted. Starting from a receptionist who crossed paths with Adil then joining his office. She was content with the alimony in the starting, but she went off the deep end when she discovered the pregnancy. She was hurt by the cheating but the pregnancy destroyed her. So, I understand the revenge track to an extent.
As for Karan, his relationship with his mother just made me angry. He was like Mrunal’s character. Always waiting for the abuser to show even a little love. 
I didn’t care for even a single episode this season. Except Vikrant and Arjun scenes. Nawab is Karan’s anchor, I will fight you if you say otherwise. I was rooting for them.
Another couple I was rooting for were Nadeem and Jazz. I felt that Nadeem understood her more than Kabir does. 
Then entire season was disappointing. 
Now I am glad that I didn’t watch Red white and royal blue before MIH.

comment: I genuinely agree that this season was very disconnected as if the characters had no connection to one another, the illogical placement of dialogues that too in English which felt so unnecessary and out of touch. author: @Sacrificallamba
comment: While agree with most of things you point out, I want to defend Karan, because I stand where he stands. It is immensely painful to watch someone you love in so much pain. And when they blame you for their pain, it’s just hard to face them. Karan does care for other people (He helps Dia Mirza’s character and the kid who misses his dad. He also stays in touch with Mitali). But he fails to open up, and he makes mommy out of Tara. His mom taught him to hide. In an environment like that, you usually don’t have the vocabulary to express anything but lie or get angry. So when Kabir asks him if he is fine or when Tara asks him what is up, he either lies and yells. Karan feels a lot. Just not for his family. He also expects Tara to fix things if he can’t do it. When his boyfriend asks him to open  up, he packs and leaves. Karan only really expresses himself to Nawab and only really holds on to Tara. And after all Karan’s mom did to him, it would be insensitive, if not outright homophobic, to expect Karan to come and help her. She didn’t accept her son, she didn’t even really love him, so she got what she deserved.

author: @simranwilasra2196
comment:
Wow, these were my exact thoughts on this season! It did lose some of the first season's charm, and I was really missing the absurdist humour from rich people being rich people, so that Bollywood wedding episode was a breath of fresh air. A couple of the episodic weddings did get too preachy, like a checklist of social issues, which is never fun. The first season brought up all those things so organically. Like the commitment ceremony shtick, it was just so tired and done and it felt so disconnected, and kind of cringe. 

But, the personal stories of the Made In Heaven team stayed interesting and entertaining. Watching Tara screw over Adil Khanna was so cathartic, just because he deserves way worse and Jim Sarbh plays a great manipulative dickhead. Karan's personal journey about his messed up relationship with his mother and his self-destructive activities to try to deal with it was heart-wrenching, and I just wanted to reach into the screen and give him a hug. Mona Singh as Bulbul Ji was so much fun, but also had a really interesting story, and the quiet support of Vijay Raaz added so much. 

I wish they did more with Meher, and saw her past her being trans, because she could have been a great new addition, but alas all of her subplots were just her being the local punching bag. Again that's tired, that's done. We queer people are more than our tragedy, and its high time Bollywood gets that.

author: @vinnienanda
comment: Honestly apart from Radhika Apte episode (though the makers could have written Apte’s episode much better) and Dia Mirza episode (honestly she should get more opportunities, her acting was so impactful and the camera work done during her emotional turmoil was WOW) rest of the episode were just either a repetition of several different movies and tv shows or just plain cringe fest. I almost felt as if I’m watching a BBC drama show where all the Indian character who aren’t familiar with Hindi in real life are speaking in English in that show. I found Adil and Faiza’s character much more sensible than that of Tara Khanna’s. Also what are the Made in Heaven team really doing to make their company profitable. Mona Singh was the only character that had a genuinely hard hitting story, especially her monologue to her son. Rest just unfortunately existed for the sake of the plot.

comment:
It was really disappointing how tara's character has shown in the end. This was not women empowering or any kind of character development , instead they decreased the dignity of her character badly and showed her a complete gold digger. If your show is so famous , you are responsible for the social impact as well. You are spreading one message from her character that poor woman only wants to marry rich man and then use his money.
I'm not saying to show her a good woman but writing could have been much better with it , her struggle in business with her personal trauma, her hard work, her love towards her boyfriend who was just a chef etc. all have been wasted But all they wanted is  to convert  this in a gold digger character, be like her mom. SAD

author: @arlenejames.
comment: I can't believe people are actually rooting for Aadil. He seems like the most despicable character for me because he continuously cheats on the women he's apparently in "love" with. To me, Raghav seemed more like a rebound for Tara after Aadil. A major reason she married Aadil was to get to the status she always craved for (and what was expected of her by her mother). She could not achieve that with Raghav and felt like she was dating "down" from what she was used to. She had to reformulate her lifestyle because of her divorce but she never stopped being power hungry. This is apparent in how she felt while interacting with Raghav's family- she notes that there's no "hidden agendas" which was a contrast to how she was brought up and how her in-laws functioned. She always (like them) functioned because of "hidden agendas".

Most annoying character for me this season was Karan's ...i ended up skipping most of his parts... randomly hooking up with anybody and everybody...then conveniently asking them for money ...and  moving on whenever it suits him...

author: @mr1nal
comment:
It was such a downfall from Season 1. Both, marriages, & Made in Heaven, took a backseat to individual character arcs (which in themselves, were shitty and/or superfluous in nature)

Do not feel any empathy for either Tara or Karan - they make wrong & morally corrupt choices - and do not take responsibility for their actions, as mature individuals should - you cannot forever go blaming circumstances & past history for your current actions! 

The different topics that they chose seemed more like a lip-service to online, intellectual junta rather than actually striking a chord - and seemed the filmmakers were themselves confused - should we do the lip-service or should we troll the same audience who is looking for such tick-box topics in any series/movie!

The only vibe of fresh air was Mona Singh - the only reason why I stuck till the last episode - to see her story!

The curse of 2nd season not living to the hype of the 1st season continues with Made in Heaven :/

comment:
@cricketclassics5490  At this point, it's like talking to a brick wall.
Beauty standards change all the time, ALL THE TIME. There was a time where stick thin body types were the beauty standard, now being "thicc" is desirable.

People can be conditioned to like something, they can be brainwashed to like something. It's all part of psychology but obviously it seems to be a very advanced topic for you to understand.

The "fair skin" obsession came in full force after colonisation, what you are perpetuating is a standard that was put on you by the people that enslaved your country, good job on that.

If I say I prefer a person who isn't a criminal to be my partner, is that the same as wanting a tall or fair person?

You want the address the root cause? You are the root cause, people like you who refuse to change and insult others for wanting to or talk about change are the root cause. 
You make assumptions about my life, call me cheap, insult me whichever way you want and then act like you are somehow right, is the root cause of the problem.

author: @ankurbagai1056
comment:
1. Absolutely disappointing, MIH is another example of 2nd season syndrome that Indian OTT & Movies generally have, they make an absolute baller of the first season. Then the hype & expectation takes over and they just completely destroy it. It happened with Sacred Games, Trippling, College Romance, and now MIH.
2. Forced Social Commentary & Wokeness this season was too over the top. Karan has random hookups & drug scenes, Tara & Adil's One Night Stand leading for her to ask for the Mansion, Mrunal Thakur's episode did somewhat of the good in the start but the end was as cliche as ever, Radhika Apte's lecture on Casteism, and Trinetra as walking & talking lecture on everything. 
3. The way they showed "Men being dumb" in Trinertra's sequence at the bar with her friends & Danish's Birthday Party was absolutely dumb. It's not 2018 or 2019, it is 2023.
4. Shahshank as Kabir being that mysterious guy just became the cliche rich family brat with out of box cliche dream. 
5. Jazz sequence with her family or with Shahshank was again so weird, and unnecessary. 
6. But the worst was Sarah Jane & Imad's Sequence & Samir Soni & Neelam Kothari's sequence like they made Sanjay Kapoor a walking talking stereotype, the wedding was of no unbelievable drama, and Sarah Jane was just like something happened but no one knows what. But the worst was Dia Mirza's sequence with Forced Two Marriage Law & Stuff. Even Ved Iyer's sequence was a bit cringe.

From Emmy Nominated to Disappointing. Maybe MIH Season 3 be of some retribution.

author: @RohTya
comment:
I found Tara’s and Karan’s journeys quite natural from where they were left by the end of season 1. Tara was always manipulative and a gold digger and heavily influenced/brainwashed by her gold digging mother and she did show the semblance of redemption or humanity when she looked after her niece and initially agreed to the terms of her divorce settlement. Throughout season 2, it can be seen how she is still so stuck in her old lifestyle and how hard she tried to fit into that world which she manipulated her way into but would never be accepted because of her background. Towards the end, it became sort of a revenge where she knows that she would never command respect because of where she comes from and wanted to hit Adil n her family where it can hurt them the most and she lost a genuine guy because of it. Infact it felt quite fitting to see that she cannot just shed off the years of who she has been, just like that and become an overall better human being. Karan had even worse because a mother who is supposed to know, understand and accept her child unconditionally is not able to accept him. His anger towards his mother broke him to disastrous levels and he could not be a bigger person to forgive her because of all the abuse he got from her. Once again, it felt quite fitting that he could not say goodbye to her and that broke him even more because the recipient of his anger is gone and he does not know how to resolve it anymore. Trinetra’s character did not work for me a lot because she had to be given more to deal with than just her sexuality. However, that also kinda made sense in some way because may be, that is all she has to deal with throughout and constantly.
I don’t really agree with ‘rooting for your protagonist’ part because not all protagonists are to be rooted for.

author: @reflectsonlife
comment:
I didn't like how the wedding planners didn't try to step in, on this episode or in any other.  That's one thing I enjoyed in S1. In S2 the wedding planners seem too politically correct, respecting a woman's agency to make her own choices, instead of warning their clients that they are about to ruin their own lives or risk their own safety. 

That being said, I have worked with domestic violence victims as a crisis volunteer.  Unfortunately too many who have the financial means to leave gaslight themselves into staying anyways. It's a phenomenon that ppl don't understand unless they faced it themselves.  Hope and Delusion are powerful forces.  My observation is that it's only when Hope dies that ppl move on, and you can't force Hope to die for people who are still clinging to it. 

Furthermore this particular episode is about a man who is like a cult leader and she is like his brainwashed cult follower succumbing to Stockholm Syndrome.  If he were an ordinary rich guy she might not have stayed, but her belief in his Jay-Shetty-ness left her in disbelief as to his real self, since the two halves were so incongruous to her.

author: @dasikakn
comment:
The sex was gratuitous in season 2. In the first season it was used to reveal something about the characters, but in the second one it was tawdry and gross. 
The individual arcs were also tiresome because the characters didn’t develop. Karan dabbling in drugs and easily quitting cold turkey is ridiculous. 
Jauhari who runs illegal loans in the back of an appliance shop is a business savant with an heart of gold who speaks english and lives in a tasteful mansion? Bruh…
Tara was also a bad story. Her becoming a stone cold society climber was not as problematic (because TBH everyone does it ), but IMO the alimony case was shown extremely poorly. Most housewives, rich or poor, are forced to accept the dalliances (and children) of their husbands because getting any settlement from the marriage is seen as “gold digging”. Why doesn’t the wife who is expected to give up her literal body and health to bear children for the husband’s family and last name get half, especially if the husband is the one who cheated? Why doesn’t the mom (Bindu) get the company control after the dad dies? 
Why doesn’t the illegitimate daughter get equal shares of the company? She is his kid too. The script missed so many opportunities to dig into some good stuff and instead chose to focus on unnecessary sex.

author: @Maya_hee
comment:
@NoNonsense_01  Not sure where from my comment did you take away the idea that all of women's preferences are justifiable.
You are projecting. Preference is one thing and its very very subjective. 

However, all these deep seated insecurities don't come out preference but an active perpetuation of a certain ideology.

Are you going to sit here and pretend that fair skin has not been a forced beauty standard?
To this day, there are ads that are about how a dark skin girl is undesirable and then she uses a cream or a bleach that makes her "beautiful".
This is called forcing a standard, this is where "force" came into the situation. Dark skinned men and women made to feel ugly in their own skin and then banking on that insecurity.

And no, you are wrong. It is basic human decency to not make people feel like they are "ugly". Sure no one is obligated to be a decent human being, right? I agree with that.

What you are talking about is a subjective preference. When you tell someone "Sorry, but you are not my type" that's fine and valid.

But when you tell someone "Sorry, you're ugly so I don't like you" or even making fun of someone's appearance is a low act no matter how you put it.

Calling someone "ugly" takes away the subjectivity of the matter and instead presents it as "facts" as if they are objectively undesirable and will never be wanted which is not true.

author: @bhavz_r
comment:
Thank you Anmol for pointing out the contrivances of the show! The dialogues felt so forced and preachy that I was put off from the very beginning. Didn't care about Tara and I wonder why the show has even tried to get the audience to root for a dedicated opportunist (this is all she does in S2 unlike S1 that shows her layers and complexities).
It's like the wedding planners had taken upon themselves to "fix" broken perceptions and relationships. Painfully so, the format of the show was social commentary with bits of story in between.
That being said, mad love for Mona, Vijay, Arjun and Vikrant-- the only characters who acted like real people. I wish we had more of Jim and Kalki too because they're simply mesmerising in every frame!

author: @ThisIsNotIt37
comment:
I'm so glad you commented on the overload of English. I had the same thought that they were catering to the International Emmys. And I was so confused why suddenly they were making Adil and Faiza so likable. Faiza was unhinged in the previous season, where she couldn't see the error of her ways of being in a relationship with a married man. And the growth they showed for Tara went down the drain; I get what they were trying to show, that she's under her mom's influence, but it's like S1 Tara didn't happen at all. Additionally, does nobody in MIH know what a committed relationship is? 

I notice this a lot in medical or law dramas. What engaged the audience in the first season, i.e .the cases, are forgotten or on the back burner for Season 2 to focus more on the characters. I get that, but it takes away the aspect that people liked about the show in the first place. Especially if your characters stopped being likable.

I was also annoyed that Meher's only character trait was that she was always looking for dates. Dating for a trans person must be very hard but I'm sure that's not the only issue they face. 

Bulbul was the best character. It must be scary for a mother to see her child becoming increasingly misogynistic. I liked that she had the moral standard to take her son to the police, which, as much as we want this to be true, would highly be unlikely in the real world. 

Even if you introduce a social message in each episode, they need to spend more time delving into it, otherwise it feels superficial and virtue-signaling.

author: @niharikasharma678
comment: This review comes as a shock to me to be honest. Yes, this time the show dealt with many issues. What’s the harm? It was done very well. There were several moments that made me emotional. Especially, the one when Vikrant Massey calls Arjun’s mother a bitch over not trying hard enough to understand his situation. And no I had no sympathy with Tara, but what she did was most natural for her character’s arc. The show doesn’t justify her deeds, and why wouldn’t one expect Faiza and Adil to be happy. They went through their personal hell to come to this point in their life. This show doesn’t portrays good people or bad people, I believe Zoya is just telling us that people have many shades. It’s impossible to put them in a box with a label.

author: @bippityboop
comment: the only star of this season was bulbul jauhari and mr jauhari. Being a die hard MIH fan and having watched season 1 way too many times to be considered a normal fan and not an obsessed one, I think this season lacked soul. The writers were so focused on humanising the (main) characters, that they made the characters ultimately bland. so black and white. They just ended up being normal people taking decisions, not leads of a tv show with stories to tell. And the brides, where were the brides who are dramatic and chaotic af? Why were the women so nice, the brides so nice? so preachy omg. Cant believe this was the same team that gave us, "this is 5000 crore babe, and the parents are eventually gonna die" line. so fucking  disappointed.

author: @awakenization
comment: I think they tried a lot and most of the perception is again handpicked from Twitter chatter rather than seeing the story as a whole. The makers had arc for each character and how they were developing. Tara is now a proper businesswomen, where their entire relationship is transactional, when she sees she isn’t getting a deal in the so called marriage business contract, she ups the game and asks for what she deserves, Karan character develops to accepting himself, the motivation to go on self destruct mode because he hates himself subconsciously because someone he loves doesn’t love him for who he is, and the entire season I think the theme was equality. I guess they followed the template to the core rather than being realistic in screenplay, many beats are conveniently placed to serve the theme than natural progression, other than that, it’s a good take and a conversation starter for many topics. I guess the makers wanted to wrap it up but I hope they still continue with the story following season 1 format.

author: @devendradceite1
comment:
I went in for a subtle nuanced show but came out of a moral science class. After 3rd Episode it became too preachy and reached its crescendo in the last episode. Also, Request film makers to not dumb down the scene by explicitly putting everything in dialog and ruining the whole scene. They should trust their viewers. A trust that believes they'll pick up on the more subtle undercurrents without having every moral or point spelled out in large, bold letters.

As for the character arcs, Tara's character trajectory is somewhat erratic, often left me grappling for clarity. One moment, she exudes joy in Raghav's company, and almost in a blink, she's berating him over his lifestyle and choices. This oscillation feels jarring. Similarly, Arjun's motivations are puzzling. His decision to leave Akshay without any solid reasoning, combined with his aimless wanderings, makes it challenging to discern his true desires or objectives in life.

author: @bedanga748
comment: Although I get what Shantanu is saying about the last story (Sarah and Imaad Shah's one), I completely understood the point it wanted to put across.  The story was more like if you know this won't work out or will have issues (as both wanted different paths in life), than don't go for a long commitment. It's rather beautiful to break up mutually and go on your own paths than to stay together and resent each other for long time. At the end of day, love will keep fluctuating in life, but what's important is to live life with someone will respect you for life (not resent you as you  might act as an obstacle).

author: @srikantsdipa
comment: I enjoyed this season too as the first one. I felt this season was more about every character's darker side as a human. In the recent times movies/series have time and again pushed the audience to ponder if the protaganist is actually the one they must be rooting for. For example Tara is a gold-digger but is she worse than Adil the prvileged brat who does not want to part away from a small part of his dad's company even though he has enough? Karan who loses complete control on his own life is still able to spot a mother-child relation being strained in his clients, does he deserve to give that gyan? To conclude I felt this season did bring out the shades of grey of humans.

author: @Maya_hee
comment:
@NoNonsense_01  You have completely derailed off of the actual point in all of this.

It is a very simple point of topic. Beauty is subjective and dark skin is not ugly.
This is literally it, all there is to it.

You're right, our worldviews are different, I am not going to get on that especially on topics such as transgender or body positivity or feminism. India, while a somewhat progressive country, is still well behind on these topics and the regressive views won't change with one youtube discussion.

When you say that someone isn't your type and they perceive it as you calling them ugly then that's not on you, I thought that was common sense. However, when you actively call someone ugly as if it's a fact and not subjective or dependant on preference then that's on you. 
Sure, stick to negative obligations, that's your personal choice, the only thing I can control is how I choose to view people with this behavior.

author: @the_artsy_words
comment:
I agree with all of your observations, esp about Meher, Tara and Bulbul's characters. But we got to cheer for Arjun as Karan cause of his acting capability. 

The series does feel like an anthology without any proper conclusion. 
It tries to do samaaj kalyaan along with the fancy wedding but fails in both. Neither the societal commentary hits hard(except a few), or the great Indian weddings. As half of the time, everyone is in a dilemma if the wedding is going to happen or not.

author: @shashnksharma2840
comment: This season of Made in Heaven is a big let down for so many reasons and on so many levels.  Firstly, its mostly in English language... speaking in English among themselves  already makes indians a bigtime laughing  stock in front of the foreigners,  the obsession of filmmakers to keep Hindi dialogues to minimum  was sooo sooo  annoying  that after a point it started feeling so artificial... forced and plastic... if i have to watch an english series, I would  rather watch something original  by native english speakers... instead of watching  these brown  skinned yet white wannabee high society  morons in india (sorry for sounding racist, but english speaking indians are infact so  annoying... one should know english but not flaunt it everywhere...!!! Bollywood Nepos... learn something  from West... they laugh at u when u speak  in english among yourselves)... Secondly,  all the issues raised in the series appeared so forced, and nothing was organic... The first season was much better in that respect... here, even issues like  sexuality, introduction of a trans-gender character  without any depth, caste issues without any depth... seemed very shallow... this show neither qualifies  as fantastical as GoT or House of Dragons... nor it seems in the totally realistic   zone ..... its basically a khichdi made to tick all boxes... to project themselves as woke and progressive... but the truth is that the story has no soul... when will indian filmmakers learn from series like  Better Call Saul, Breaking Bad, succession, White Lotus, Only murders in the building, Komisky Method... that there is a lot beyond just making statements without any soul..  no character development... nothing..  a big thumbs down!!!

author: @shashnksharma2840
comment: This season of Made in Heaven is a big let down for so many reasons and on so many levels.  Firstly, its mostly in English language... speaking in English among themselves  already makes indians a bigtime laughing  stock in front of the foreigners,  the obsession of filmmakers to keep Hindi dialogues to minimum  was sooo sooo  annoying  that after a point it started feeling so artificial... forced and plastic... if i have to watch an english series, I would  rather watch something original  by native english speakers... instead of watching  these brown  skinned yet white wannabee high society  morons in india (sorry for sounding racist, but english speaking indians are infact so  annoying... one should know english but not flaunt it everywhere...!!! Bollywood Nepos... learn something  from the West... they laugh at u when u speak  in english among yourselves)... Secondly,  all the issues raised in the series appeared so forced, and nothing was organic... The first season was much better in that respect... here, even issues like  sexuality, introduction of a trans-gender character  without any depth, caste issues without any depth... seemed very shallow... this show neither qualifies  as fantastical as GoT or House of Dragons... nor it seems in the totally realistic   zone ..... its basically a khichdi made to tick all boxes... to project themselves as woke and progressive... but the truth is that the story has no soul... when will indian filmmakers learn from series like  Better Call Saul, Breaking Bad, succession, White Lotus, Only murders in the building, Komisky Method... that there is a lot beyond just making statements without any soul..  no character development... nothing..  a big thumbs down!!!

author: @alisha6227
comment: I couldn’t agree more. The entire season felt like the last season of GOT where the makers felt forced to put something out and in the end killed the very seeds of emotion and connection they had planted in the previous seasons. I wasn’t expecting to empathise with Tara and felt they kept her true to form with shades of grey but the whole new bf and love for MIL arc was such a waste. Arjun in true Arjun form presses self destruct repeatedly and after seeing it so many times you just get anaesthetised to his pain. His healthy bf though this season was a breath of fresh air. The only episode that was worth the watch was Mrunal and Apte’s where you feel some connection to the character and the social issue. Unfortunately the worst episode was the last which leaves you so disappointed with the makers just trying to give everything a happy ending of sorts to everyone and just close the season for lack of a better ending. They’d created a beautifully flawed world in season 1 but eventually probably lost interest and it shows. Mona Singh and Vijay Raza were the only characters worth watching but towards the end they rushed Mona’s story arc too which was annoying. They wasted a lot of screen time on irrelevant sexual scenes which added nothing to the story telling. The Tamil - punjabi divorcee wedding with the depressed kid was so random it felt like it was just forced In the there to tick another box.Only positives were probably some of the outfits and wedding decor and Mona Singh.

author: @shashnksharma2840
comment: This season of Made in Heaven is a big let down for so many reasons and on so many levels.  Firstly, its mostly in English language... speaking in English among themselves  already makes indians a bigtime laughing  stock in front of the foreigners,  the obsession of filmmakers to keep Hindi dialogues to minimum  was sooo sooo  annoying  that after a point it started feeling so artificial... forced and plastic... if i have to watch an english series, I would  rather watch something original  by native english speakers... instead of watching  these brown  skinned yet white wannabee high society  morons in india (sorry for sounding racist, but english speaking indians are infact so  annoying... one should know english but not flaunt it everywhere...!!! Bollywood Nepos... learn something  from the West... they laugh at u when u speak  in english among yourselves)... Secondly,  all the issues raised in the series appeared so forced, and nothing was organic... The first season was much better in that respect... here, even issues like  sexuality, introduction of a trans-gender character  without any depth, caste issues without any depth... seemed very shallow... this show neither qualifies  as fantastical as GoT or House of Dragons... nor it seems in the totally realistic   zone ..... its basically a khichdi made to tick all boxes... to project themselves as woke and progressive... but the truth is that the story has no soul... when will indian filmmakers learn from series like  Better Call Saul, Breaking Bad, succession, White Lotus, Only murders in the building, Komisky Method... that there is a lot beyond just making statements without any soul..  no character development... nothing..  a big thumbs down!!!!

author: @virajkapoor5058
comment:
Expect for Bulbul ji and Mehar’s storyline, it’s like you’re stuck in the constant loop of some unnecessary dilemmas. I absolutely adore the idea of coming up with complex issues involving each of the couples, but when you’ve had character development stories for each of MIH employees, you rather focus on the aspects of them working as a team to do what they do, and that’s plan a fucking wedding. 

Every episode you have Karan or Tara pitching an idea, the bride and groom immediately accepting it and then cut to them asking the same question in every episode - “ab yeh shaadi hogi na?”

After an absolute beauty of a first episode, the show just feels like that piece of content that raises questions on what it could’ve easily been - a comfort flick that teaches, but it simply becomes a mixture of everything that it didn’t need to be.

comment: I can just take a moment to admire how beautiful the shots were in the series, I couldn't take my eyes off the screen for a second, but when it comes to characters I had some problems, Vijay Raaz is such a great actor was excited to see him at the end of first season but in season two he barely had any screen time, some of Tara's actions were questionable but then since growing up her mother groomed her to be a gold digger, also in karan's case i understand it's hard to deal with parent who doesn't accept you but atleast during her dying time he could have helped his dad and brother, it's very exhausting to be in the hospital and to take care of your family. author: @Pk5714-
comment: I can't believe the fact that people perceive Tara's character as "grey" and Adil as "unstable". Adil has commitment issues, is a serial cheater and is a shitty person to his sister(I'd be too if I suddenly found out that my own family has been keeping such secrets and dumps it at me in the worst possible way so that's understandable) but nothing he does is even remotely illegal or evil but it's not the same for Tara, she does evil and illegal things left, right and centre but people always justify her actions because of her upbringing. I despise that. The same applies to most of the female characters in the show, when a male character does something terrible, everyone hates it but when a female character does the same thing/more, no one bats an eye.
